prompts: Buoyancy keeps ships afloat due to Archimedes' Principle, which states that an object displaces a volume of water equal to its own weight. Ships stay buoyant as they are designed to displace enough water to balance their own weight, thus preventing them from sinking. Their structure incorporates large hollow spaces filled with air, reducing overall density below that of water. Despite being made of heavy materials, this low density ensures they float. Understanding these principles offers insights into ship design and naval engineering, highlighting the fascinating interplay of physics that enables their journey across vast oceans.
